preamble.summary: Psalm 1 serves as the gateway to the entire Psalter, establishing the fundamental contrast that runs through all 150 psalms: the way of the righteous versus the way of the wicked. It is a wisdom psalm that draws its categories directly from the torah tradition, presenting life as a binary choice between two paths with two destinies.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm contains no prayer, no praise, no lament — it is pure instruction. Its placement at the head of the Psalter transforms the entire collection from a hymnbook into a curriculum. The reader is told before singing a single song: the person who meditates on the LORD's instruction day and night will flourish. The Psalter begins not with worship but with the demand to study. The tree metaphor in verse 3 is not decorative but structural — a tree planted by water channels does not choose to bear fruit; it bears fruit because of where it is rooted. Location determines outcome. The righteous person's fruitfulness is not achievement but consequence.
preamble.friction: The Hebrew ashre ('happy, blessed, fortunate') that opens the psalm is not a blessing pronounced by God but a declaration of observed reality — closer to 'how fortunate is' than 'blessed be.' The psalm's sharp binary between righteous and wicked leaves no room for moral ambiguity, which reflects wisdom literature's tendency toward clean categories. Real life is messier, and the rest of the Psalter — especially the lament psalms — will complicate this tidy framework considerably.
preamble.connections: The opening word ashre links Psalm 1 to Psalm 2:12 ('ashre kol chose vo,' 'happy are all who take refuge in him'), forming a literary bracket around the paired introduction to the Psalter. The tree-by-water image echoes Jeremiah 17:7-8, which uses nearly identical language. The 'chaff driven by wind' imagery appears in Isaiah 17:13 and Hosea 13:3. The torah meditation theme connects to Joshua 1:8, where the same verb hagah ('meditate, murmur') and the same promise of success appear in God's charge to Joshua.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֥שְֽׁרֵי־הָאִ֗ישׁ אֲשֶׁ֤ר ׀ לֹ֥א הָלַךְ֮ בַּעֲצַ֢ת רְשָׁ֫עִ֥ים וּבְדֶ֣רֶךְ חַ֭טָּאִים לֹ֥א עָמָ֑ד וּבְמוֹשַׁ֥ב לֵ֝צִ֗ים לֹ֣א יָשָֽׁב׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful.
verses.rendering:
How fortunate is the person
who has not walked in the counsel of the wicked,
nor stood in the path of sinners,
nor sat in the seat of scoffers.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with ashre — not a prayer but an observation: 'how fortunate, how enviable.' Three verbs trace a downward trajectory of moral compromise: halakh ('walked'), amad ('stood'), yashav ('sat'). Walking suggests passing contact; standing suggests lingering; sitting suggests settling in. Three nouns match the descent: resha'im ('wicked'), chatta'im ('sinners'), letsim ('scoffers'). The wicked disregard God's order; sinners miss the mark actively; scoffers mock anyone who takes righteousness seriously. The righteous person is defined first by negation — by what they refuse to do, by the company they decline to keep.
translator_notes: The three-stage progression (walk, stand, sit) is a deliberate rhetorical intensification. Each verb represents deeper entrenchment in a way of life opposed to God. The progression from counsel (atsah) to path (derekh) to seat (moshav) mirrors the movement from occasional influence to habitual practice to permanent residence.

Ashre is a plural construct — literally 'happinesses of' — functioning as an exclamation. It appears 26 times in the Psalter and always describes a state of flourishing that results from right relationship with God or right conduct.

key_terms.semantic_range: happy, blessed, fortunate, enviable; a state of flourishing
key_terms.note: ashre is not a divine blessing (berakhah) but a human observation of well-being. It declares a person's state rather than invoking God's action. The word frames both Psalm 1:1 and Psalm 2:12, bracketing the Psalter's introduction.

key_terms.semantic_range: wicked, guilty, condemned, those in the wrong; those who violate covenant order
key_terms.note: resha'im (from rasha, 'to be wicked, to act wickedly') are not merely bad people but those who actively oppose the moral order God has established. They stand in direct contrast to the tsaddiq ('righteous one') throughout the Psalter. The term carries legal overtones — the rasha is the guilty party in a dispute.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י אִ֥ם בְּתוֹרַ֥ת יְהוָ֗ה חֶ֫פְצ֥וֹ וּבְתוֹרָת֥וֹ יֶהְגֶּ֗ה יוֹמָ֥ם וָלָֽיְלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: But his delight is in the law of the LORD; and in his law doth he meditate day and night.
verses.rendering:
Rather, his delight is in the instruction of the LORD,
and on His instruction he meditates day and night.
verses.expanded_rendering: After the triple negation of verse 1, the positive definition arrives: the fortunate person's chefets ('delight, desire, pleasure') is in the torah of the LORD. Torah here does not mean 'the Law' in a legalistic sense — it means instruction, teaching, guidance, the full weight of God's revealed will. The verb yehgeh (from hagah) means to murmur, to recite in a low voice, to turn over repeatedly — it describes the practice of speaking a text aloud to oneself, letting it fill the mind. Yomam va-laylah ('day and night') is a merism meaning 'at all times, without ceasing.' The righteous person is not someone who occasionally consults God's instruction but someone whose inner life is saturated with it.
translator_notes: Torah in this context predates the later identification of Torah with the Pentateuch. Here it encompasses any authoritative instruction from God — wisdom, commandment, prophetic word. We render it 'instruction' rather than 'law' to avoid the misleading implication that the psalmist delights in legal code.

The verb hagah appears in Joshua 1:8 in the identical phrase: 'you shall meditate on it day and night.' The parallel suggests that Psalm 1 consciously echoes the charge given to Joshua as Israel's leader — the Psalter's ideal reader takes on a Joshua-like posture before God's word.

key_terms.semantic_range: instruction, teaching, law, direction, guidance; from yarah ('to throw, to cast, to direct')
key_terms.note: Torah's root yarah means 'to point, to direct, to shoot (an arrow).' At its core, torah is direction — God pointing the way. Rendering it 'law' imports Greco-Roman legal categories that flatten the Hebrew concept. In Psalm 1, torah is the object of delight, not obligation — the righteous person is drawn to it, not burdened by it.

key_terms.semantic_range: to murmur, mutter, recite, growl, moan, meditate; vocalized reflection
key_terms.note: hagah is not silent contemplation but audible recitation. In Joshua 1:8, the same verb describes speaking God's instruction aloud. In Isaiah 31:4, a lion hagah-s over its prey — it growls. The word conveys intensity and repetition: the righteous person chews on God's word, turning it over vocally and mentally.

verses.text_hebrew: וְֽהָיָ֗ה כְּעֵץ֮ שָׁת֢וּל עַֽל־פַּלְגֵ֫י מָ֥יִם אֲשֶׁ֤ר פִּרְי֨וֹ ׀ יִתֵּ֬ן בְּעִתּ֗וֹ וְעָלֵ֥הוּ לֹֽא־יִבּ֑וֹל וְכֹ֖ל אֲשֶׁר־יַעֲשֶׂ֣ה יַצְלִֽיחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: And he shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water, that bringeth forth his fruit in his season; his leaf also shall not wither; and whatsoever he doeth shall prosper.
verses.rendering:
He will be like a tree
transplanted beside channels of water,
which yields its fruit in its season
and whose leaf does not wither.
Everything he does will thrive.
verses.expanded_rendering: The simile shifts from human action to botanical reality. The word shatul ('transplanted, planted') suggests deliberate placement — someone moved this tree to the water's edge. It did not grow there by accident. The palge mayim ('channels of water') are irrigation ditches, human-made watercourses that ensure a constant supply. Three results follow: the tree gives fruit be-itto ('in its proper season' — not forced, not premature, but at the right time); its leaf does not wither (naval, 'to wilt, to decay, to fall away'); and everything the person does yatsliach ('succeeds, thrives, prospers'). The metaphor's logic is simple but profound: rootedness in God's instruction produces fruitfulness the way proximity to water produces growth. The person does not strain to produce results; results flow from position.
translator_notes: We render shatul as 'transplanted' rather than 'planted' because the word implies relocation — the tree was moved from one place to another and deliberately set beside water. This carries theological weight: the righteous person has been repositioned by choice or by God's action into a place of nourishment.

The verb yatsliach ('will prosper, will succeed') in the final clause shifts the metaphor from the tree back to the person. Jeremiah 17:7-8 uses almost identical imagery but adds the detail that such a tree 'does not fear when heat comes' — an expansion of the same metaphor to include resilience under pressure.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־כֵ֥ן הָרְשָׁעִ֑ים כִּ֥י אִם־כַּ֝מֹּ֗ץ אֲֽשֶׁר־תִּדְּפֶ֥נּוּ רֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: The ungodly are not so: but are like the chaff which the wind driveth away.
verses.rendering:
Not so the wicked —
they are like chaff
that the wind drives away.
verses.expanded_rendering: The contrast is stark and abrupt. Lo khen ha-resha'im ('not so the wicked') — three words that demolish any pretense of permanence. Against the deep-rooted, fruit-bearing, leaf-keeping tree, the psalmist sets mots ('chaff') — the dry, weightless husks separated from grain on the threshing floor. Chaff has no root, no substance, no future. The wind (ruach) does not merely move it but tidfennu ('drives it away, scatters it'). The same ruach that can mean 'spirit' or 'breath of God' here functions as the agent of removal. The wicked have no anchor, no depth, no staying power. They are waste material — what is left over after the valuable grain has been collected.
translator_notes: The brevity of this verse is itself a rhetorical statement. The tree of verse 3 received elaborate description — transplanted, watered, fruitful, leafy, prospering. The wicked receive three words of metaphor: chaff, wind, gone. The asymmetry communicates that wickedness has no substance worth describing at length.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־כֵּ֤ן ׀ לֹא־יָקֻ֣מוּ רְ֭שָׁעִים בַּמִּשְׁפָּ֑ט וְ֝חַטָּאִ֗ים בַּעֲדַ֥ת צַדִּיקִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore the ungodly shall not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous.
verses.rendering:
Therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment,
nor sinners in the assembly of the righteous.
verses.expanded_rendering: Al ken ('therefore, for this reason') draws the conclusion. The verb yaqumu ('will stand, will rise') carries legal force — to stand in judgment is to be vindicated, to have one's case upheld. The wicked lo yaqumu ba-mishpat: they will not be able to stand when judgment comes, because they have no substance to support them. The parallel line extends the exclusion: sinners will not be found ba-adat tsaddiqim ('in the assembly of the righteous'). The edah ('assembly, congregation') is the gathered community of those who belong to God. The wicked are excluded not by arbitrary decree but by the nature of things — chaff cannot remain on the threshing floor once the wind comes.
translator_notes: The mishpat ('judgment') here may refer to God's ongoing governance of moral reality rather than a single eschatological event. The psalm's perspective is that the moral order is self-executing: the wicked do not endure because wickedness has no structural integrity.

The tsaddiqim ('righteous ones') appear here for the first time in the Psalter — the positive counterpart to the resha'im introduced in verse 1. The entire psalm builds toward this contrast: two categories, two destinies, no middle ground.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteous, just, innocent, in the right; those who conform to God's covenant order
key_terms.note: tsaddiq (from tsedaqah, 'righteousness') describes a person who is in right relationship with God and with the community. It is the direct antonym of rasha ('wicked'). In the Psalter, the tsaddiq is not morally perfect but aligned with God's purposes and dependent on God's faithfulness.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־יוֹדֵ֣עַ יְ֭הוָה דֶּ֣רֶךְ צַדִּיקִ֑ים וְדֶ֖רֶךְ רְשָׁעִ֣ים תֹּאבֵֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: For the LORD knoweth the way of the righteous: but the way of the ungodly shall perish.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD watches over the way of the righteous,
but the way of the wicked will perish.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends with a theological foundation for everything that preceded it. The verb yodea ('knows') means far more than cognitive awareness — in Hebrew, to know is to attend to, to care for, to be in relationship with. When the LORD 'knows' the derekh ('way, path, manner of life') of the righteous, He is actively guarding it, sustaining it, walking alongside it. The contrast is devastating in its simplicity: the way of the righteous is known by God; the way of the wicked toved ('will perish, will be destroyed, will come to nothing'). The wicked path does not merely fail — it ceases to exist. The verb avad ('to perish') is final: the road itself disappears. Two ways, two outcomes, one God who makes the difference.
translator_notes: We render yodea as 'watches over' rather than 'knows' to capture the active, caring dimension of Hebrew knowledge. God's knowing is never passive observation — it is covenantal attention. The same verb describes God 'knowing' Israel in Amos 3:2: 'You only have I known of all the families of the earth.'

The psalm ends where it began — with the two ways. Derekh ('way') appeared in verse 1 as the path of sinners; now it appears twice as the governing metaphor for life's trajectory. The Psalter's opening word is ashre ('fortunate'); its theological claim is that fortune belongs to those whose way God knows.
